perturbation.original_text: i. Project Intellectual Property : (“ Project IP ”), IP created during and as a direct result of work undertaken for a Project. For clarity, Project IP d oes not include Background IP.
perturbation.changed_text: i. Project Intellectual Property : (“ Project IP ”), IP created during and as a direct result of work undertaken for a Project. For clarity, Project IP includes Background IP when the Background IP directly contributes to the creation of the Project IP.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly excludes Background IP from Project IP. The modified text includes Background IP under specific conditions, thus redefining Project IP in a way that could contradict standard legal interpretations and create uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Patent law dictates that inventions must be novel and non-obvious. By including Background IP into Project IP, it may create confusion on which part of the invention the sponsor has ownership on, especially if Background IP was not novel. This modification could lead to disputes over patent ownership and inventorship if the Background IP was already known or obvious, potentially invalidating a patent claim on the 'Project IP' as a whole.

Inventions patentable Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent therefor, subject to the conditions and requirements of this title. ( July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 797 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on Title 35, U.S.C., 1946 ed., §31 (R.S. 4886, amended (1) Mar. 3, 1897, ch. 391, §1, 29 Stat. 692 , (2) May 23, 1
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 101 - Inventions patentable | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 101 - Inventions patentable U.S. Code Notes prev | next Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process , machine, manufacture, or composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent therefor, subject to the conditions and requirements of this title. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 797 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on Title 35, U.S.C., 1946 ed., § 31 (R.S. 4886, amended (1) Mar. 3, 1897, ch. 391, § 1, 29 Stat. 692 , (2) May 23, 1930, ch. 312, § 1, 46 Stat. 376 , (3) Aug. 5, 1939, ch. 450, § 1, 53 Stat. 1212 ). The corresponding section of existing statute is split into two sections, section 101 relating to the subject matter for which patents may be obtained, and section 102 defining statutory novelty and stating other conditions for patentability. Section 101 follows the wording of the existing statute as to the subject matter for patents, except that reference to plant patents has been omitted for incorporation in section 301 and the word “art” has been replaced by “ process ”, which is defined in section 100. The word “art” in the corresponding section of the existing statute has a different meaning than the same word as used in other places in the statute; it has been interpreted by the courts as being practically synonymous with process or method. “ Process ” has been used as its meaning is more readily grasped than “art” as interpreted, and the definition in section 100(b) makes it clear that “ process or method” is meant. The remainder of the definition clarifies the status of processes or methods which involve merely the new use of a known process , machine, manufacture, composition of matter, or material; they are processes or methods under the statute and may be patented provided the conditions for patentability are sa
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'Project IP' to include 'Background IP' under certain conditions, which contradicts the general understanding that Background IP is separate. The Law Explanation suggests this could create disputes over patent ownership, potentially invalidating patent claims if the Background IP was already known or obvious. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2, referencing 35 USC 101, state that 'Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process...may obtain a patent therefor...' By including pre-existing Background IP as part of Project IP, it undermines the 'new' requirement of patentability because part of the 'Project IP' is not new, which can create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: j. Confidential Information : any information disclosed by one Party (the ‘ Discloser’ ) to another party (the ‘ Recipient’ ) relating directly or indirectly to the Project, w hich is identified by the disclosing Party, either orally or in writing, as c onfidential, either at the time of disclosure or, i f disclosed orally, confirmed in writing within thirt y (30) days following the original disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: j. Confidential Information : any information disclosed by one Party (the ‘ Discloser’ ) to another party (the ‘ Recipient’ ) relating directly or indirectly to the Project, which is presumed confidential regardless of whether identified as such at the time of disclosure, unless explicitly stated otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition requires explicit identification of information as confidential. The modified version implies confidentiality unless stated otherwise. This shift of burden could contradict trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Implying confidentiality without any effort to identify it as such may not meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to claim trade secret protection. This could undermine trade secret claims under the UTSA.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract shifts the burden of identifying confidential information, presuming all information is confidential unless explicitly stated otherwise. This contradicts the UTSA's requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, as pointed out by the Law Explanation. The original contract required explicit identification, aligning better with demonstrating such efforts. The change introduces a scenario where no effort is required to designate something as confidential, weakening the potential for trade secret protection as the efforts made to keep the information secret are diminished by this change.

perturbation.original_text: The Sponsor may, within fifteen (15) days following receipt of a copy of the proposed publication require by written noti ce to McGill and the Intern that the proposed publication date be delayed, for a period of not gr eater than sixty (60) days, so as to permit the Sponsor to make an application to register a patent with respect to the Project IP.
perturbation.changed_text: The Sponsor may, indefinitely, require by written notice to McGill and the Intern that the proposed publication date be delayed to assess the commercial potential of the Project IP.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows for a maximum 60-day delay for patent application. The modified clause allows indefinite delays. This is a contradiction, as laws dictate a deadline to file for a patent.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts patent law regarding statutory bars to patentability. Specifically, 35 U.S.C. § 102 dictates that an inventor may lose the right to patent an invention if it is described in a printed publication more than one year before the date of the application for patent. The modification allows for indefinite delays, potentially delaying publication for over a year. This would destroy the novelty of the IP.

Conditions for patentability; novelty (a) Novelty; Prior Art .-A person shall be entitled to a patent unless- (1) the claimed invention was patented, described in a printed publication, or in public use, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date of the claimed invention; or (2) the claimed invention was described in a patent issued und
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Novelty; Prior Art.— A person shall be entitled to a patent unless— (1) the claimed invention was patented, described in a printed publication, or in public use, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date of the claimed invention; or (2) the claimed invention was described in a patent issued under section 151, or in an application for patent published or deemed published under section 122(b), in which the patent or application, as the case may be, names another inventor and was effectively filed before the effective filing date of the claimed invention. (b) Exceptions.— (1) Disclosures made 1 year or less before the effective filing date of the claimed invention.— A disclosure made 1 year or less before the effective filing date of a claimed invention shall not be prior art to the claimed invention under subsection (a)(1) if— (A) the disclosure was made by the inventor or joint inventor or by another who obtained the subject matter disclosed directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor ; or (B) the subject matter disclosed had, before such disclosure, been publicly disclosed by the inventor or a joint inventor or another who obtained the subject matter disclosed directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor . (2) Disclosures appearing in applications and patents.— A disclosure shall not be prior art to a claimed invention under subsection (a)(2) if— (A) the subject matter disclosed was obtained directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor ; (B) the subject matter disclosed had, before such subject matter was effectively filed under subsection (a)(2), been publicly disclosed by the inventor or a joint inventor
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts patent law. The original text allows a delay of up to 60 days for a patent application, whereas the changed text allows for indefinite delays. This contradicts 35 U.S.C. § 102, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which states that a person is entitled to a patent unless the invention was described in a printed publication before the effective filing date. Indefinite delays could lead to publication more than one year before the patent application, thus destroying the novelty of the IP as stated in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Each party must comply with all applicable laws, regulations and rules in its jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party must comply with all applicable laws, regulations and rules in its jurisdiction, except when such compliance hinders the commercialization of Project IP.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration introduces an exception to legal compliance based on commercial interests. This fundamentally contradicts the principle of adherence to the law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian Criminal Code, RSC 1985, c C-46, s 19
perturbation.law_explanation: This directly contradicts fundamental principles of legal compliance. Section 19 of the Canadian Criminal Code states that ignorance of the law is no excuse. This modified term suggests that a party can choose to disregard laws if they believe it will help commercialize Project IP. Criminal law applies irrespective of any potential profit one would make from its violation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Criminal Code (Canada) - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Comprehensive criminal law of Canada This article needs additional citations for verification . Please help improve this article by adding citations to reliable sources . Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. Find sources: "Criminal Code" Canada – news · newspapers · books · scholar · JSTOR ( September 2015 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) Criminal Code Parliament of Canada Long title An Act respecting the Criminal Law Citation R.S.C 1985, c. C-46 Territorial extent Canada [ a ] Enacted by Parliament of Canada Enacted First enacted: SC 1892, c 29; carried forward in statute revisions, RSC 1906, c 146 and RSC 1927, c 36; substantially revised and re-enacted, SC 1953-54, c 51; carried forward in statute revisions, RSC 1970, c C-34 and RSC 1985, c C-46 Assented to July 9, 1892 Commenced July 1, 1893 The Criminal Code ( French : Code criminel ) is a law of the Parliament of Canada that codifies most, but not all, criminal offences [ b ] and criminal procedure [ c ] in Canada . Its official long title is An Act respecting the Criminal Law (French: Loi concernant le droit criminel ). It is indexed in the Revised Statutes of Canada, 1985 as chapter number C-46 [ 1 ] and it is sometimes abbreviated as Cr.C. (French: C.Cr. ) in legal reports. [ 2 ] Section 91(27) of the Constitution Act, 1867 establishes that the Parliament of Canada has sole jurisdiction over criminal law . Accordingly, the Criminal Code applies to the entirety of the country, meaning that in Canada, all crimes which are defined under the Criminal Code are federal crimes and can be prosecuted anywhere they occur in or out of the country. The Criminal Code contains some defences, but most are part of the common law rather than statute . Important Canadian criminal laws not forming part of the Code include the Firearms Act , [ 3 ] the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act , [ 4 ] the Canada Evidence Act , the Food and Drugs Act , the Youth Criminal Justice Act , the Customs Act , and the Contraventions Act . The Code underwent a major revision in 1954, which came into force in April 1955, but nonetheless remains the fundamental criminal law of Canada, despite several initiatives at major reform or the enactment of a new criminal code entirely. [ 5 ] [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] In 2018, and later 2019, the Trudeau government made a large revision to the Code which repealed numerous unconstitutional or archaic offences that had remained in it up to that po
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to legal compliance based on commercial interests, allowing parties to disregard laws if they believe it will help commercialize Project IP. This contradicts the principle of adherence to the law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states 'Ignorance of the law by a person who commits an offence is not an excuse for committing that offence,' which directly conflicts with the modified text that suggests non-compliance is permissible under certain conditions. The 'Misaligned Terminology' perturbation indicates that the contract now uses terms that deviate from legally recognized definitions, rendering it potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: In no event will McGill or Intern be liable for an y special, incidental or consequential damages of any kind wha tsoever resulting from the disclosure, use or recei pt of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In no event will McGill or Intern be liable for any special, incidental or consequential damages of any kind whatsoever resulting from the disclosure, use or receipt of the Confidential Information, unless such damages arise from gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.explanation: This adds an exception to the limitation of liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct. While seemingly reasonable, it creates ambiguity regarding the standard of care and what constitutes gross negligence, potentially conflicting with established legal precedents.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1474 of the Civil Code of Quebec addresses exclusion or limitation of liability. While it permits such clauses, there are implied limitations around bad faith or intentional fault. By adding "unless such damages arise from gross negligence or willful misconduct," the contract introduces a concept ('gross negligence') that can be interpreted differently than 'intentional fault' under Quebec law, potentially creating uncertainty and grounds for challenging the liability waiver's enforceability.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the limitation of liability, specifically for damages arising from gross negligence or willful misconduct. This modification, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation, introduces ambiguity regarding the standard of care. The Law Explanation further elaborates on this, stating that the term 'gross negligence' can be interpreted differently than 'intentional fault' under Quebec law, potentially creating uncertainty and grounds for challenging the liability waiver's enforceability. Given that the change introduces a term ('gross negligence') that can be interpreted differently than the standards typically applied to limitation of liability clauses, it creates a 'Misaligned Terminology' Legal Contradiction.
